message.content:
Implement the following plan:

# ASG Documentation and Integration Test Plan

## Overview

Create comprehensive end-user documentation for ASG (API Specification Generator), then use that documentation to drive creation of exhaustive integration tests with purpose-built test ontologies.

## Phase 1: Documentation

### Directory Structure

```
docs/
├── index.md                    # Landing page and navigation
├── getting-started.md          # Quick start (5-minute setup)
├── cli-reference.md            # Command-line options
├── configuration/
│   ├── index.md               # Config overview
│   ├── config-reference.md    # All YAML options
│   ├── templates.md           # OpenAPI templates
│   └── security.md            # Security configuration
├── ontology-design/
│   ├── index.md               # Ontology requirements
│   ├── structure.md           # Required structure (APIRoot, etc.)
│   ├── annotations.md         # All supported annotations
│   ├── restrictions.md        # OWL restrictions → OpenAPI
│   └── datatypes.md           # XSD → OpenAPI type mapping
├── mapping-reference/
│   ├── owl-to-openapi-matrix.md  # Quick reference matrix
│   └── property-mappings.md      # Data/Object/Taxonomy properties
├── examples/
│   ├── minimal-example.md     # Simplest working example
│   └── common-patterns.md     # Recipe-style patterns
└── troubleshooting.md         # Common issues and solutions
```

### Key Documentation Content

#### Mapping and Ontology To an API
1. **API How to identify an API**
  - use APIRoot
  - subclass of hasAPI some `api-name`
  - If more than one APIRoot in ontology, see rootClass in config
2. ** How to specify resources in the API **
  - Resource as in REsource/REpresentational State Transfer
  - Each such class becomes a resource in the Open API spec
  - Resource classes marked by being subclass of apiID annotated with apiLabel `api-name`
  - Instances of the class in a graph will have an `apiID` data-property to uniquely identify them
  - Produces collection endpoints /`resource-name` with methods:
    - POST: create new
    - GET: list existing (with pagination)
    - Produces single item endpoints /`resource-name`/`apiID` with methods:
    - PUT: update by replacing
    - PATCH: update by merging
    - GET: fetch
    - DELETE: remove
  - Each resource class will have a schema in components/schemas
3. ** How to Specify Resource Properties
  - Data and Object properties annotated with apiLabel `api-name` are candidates
  - Restrictions specify cardinality etc in json schema (open api schema components)
  - Individuals become enums
1. **Configuration Reference** - Document all YamlConfig fields:
   - `name`, `ontologies`, `outputDir` (required)
   - `extends`, `rootClass`, `catalogFile`, `enforceRequiredness`
   - `namespaceMappings`, `securitySchemeName`, `securityTemplate`
   - `templates.openapi`, `templates.collectionPathItem`, `templates.singlePathItem`
   - Use the existing documentation in README.md as a starting point and add the configuration reference to this document

2. **OWL Restrictions** - Document all 16 RestrictionInfo types:
   - Object: MinCardinality, MaxCardinality, ExactCardinality, SomeValuesFrom, AllValuesFrom, HasValue, OneOf
   - Data: DataMinCardinality, DataMaxCardinality, DataExactCardinality, DataSomeValuesFrom, DataAllValuesFrom, DataHasValue, DataOneOf
   - Boolean: UnionOf, IntersectionOf, ComplementOf

3. **XSD Datatypes** - Document all 46 type mappings from DataPropertyMapper

4. **Annotations** - Document apiLabel, apiDescription, apiSummary, apiTag, apiID, security, skos:prefLabel, skos:definition, skos:example

### Critical Source Files for Documentation

- `/home/mike/grl/ASG/src/main/java/com/grl/component/asg/configuration/YamlConfig.java` - All config options
- `/home/mike/grl/ASG/src/main/java/com/grl/component/schema/RestrictionInfo.java` - 16 restriction types
- `/home/mike/grl/ASG/src/main/java/com/grl/component/mapper/DataPropertyMapper.java` - XSD mappings
- `/home/mike/grl/ASG/src/main/java/com/grl/component/asg/OntologyConstants.java` - Annotation IRIs

---

## Phase 2: Integration Tests

### Test Organization

```
src/test/java/regression/features/
├── restrictions/
│   ├── CardinalityRestrictionsIT.java    # Min/Max/Exact cardinality
│   ├── ValueRestrictionsIT.java          # SomeValuesFrom, AllValuesFrom, etc.
│   └── BooleanRestrictionsIT.java        # UnionOf, IntersectionOf, ComplementOf
├── datatypes/
│   ├── StringDatatypesIT.java            # string, anyURI, base64Binary, etc.
│   ├── NumericDatatypesIT.java           # integer, decimal, float, double
│   └── TemporalDatatypesIT.java          # date, dateTime, time, duration
├── properties/
│   ├── DataPropertyIT.java               # Data property scenarios
│   ├── ObjectPropertyIT.java             # $ref, x-grl-model-link
│   └── TaxonomyPropertyIT.java           # Enum from individuals
├── configuration/
│   ├── ConfigInheritanceIT.java          # extends, variable substitution
│   └── SecurityConfigIT.java             # OAuth2, API key, scopes
└── annotations/
    └── AnnotationMappingIT.java          # All annotation types
```

### Test Ontologies (Purpose-Built)

```
src/test/resources/regression/
├── ontologies/
│   ├── cardinality-test.owl              # All 6 cardinality restrictions
│   ├── value-restrictions-test.owl       # SomeValuesFrom, AllValuesFrom, etc.
│   ├── boolean-combinations-test.owl     # UnionOf, IntersectionOf, ComplementOf
│   ├── string-datatypes-test.owl         # All string-family XSD types
│   ├── numeric-datatypes-test.owl        # All numeric XSD types
│   ├── temporal-datatypes-test.owl       # date, dateTime, time, duration
│   ├── object-property-test.owl          # $ref, arrays, x-grl-model-link
│   ├── taxonomy-test.owl                 # Named individuals as enums
│   └── annotation-test.owl               # All annotation types
├── config_base.yaml                  # all extend from this (sas in existing tests)
└── configs/
    └── config_<feature>.yaml             # One config per ontology
```

### Test Patterns (Follow Existing)

From `SimpleApiIT.java:28-30`:
```java
ReadContext spec = TestUtil.getGeneratedSpec(
    TestUtil.asFilePath(FeatureIT.class, "config_feature.yaml"));
```

JSONPath assertions:
```java
assertEquals("expected", spec.read("$.components.schemas.Resource.properties.field.type"));
assertEquals(1, spec.read("$.components.schemas.Resource.properties.field.minItems", Integer.class));
```

### Coverage Matrix (High Priority Tests)

| Feature | OpenAPI Assertion |
|---------|-------------------|
| MinCardinality(n) | `minItems: n`, required if n>=1 |
| MaxCardinality(n) | `maxItems: n` |
| ExactCardinality(n) | `minItems: n, maxItems: n` |
| SomeValuesFrom | `minItems: 1`, required |
| AllValuesFrom | Type constraint only, no cardinality |
| DataOneOf | `enum: [values]` |
| DataHasValue | `default: value` |
| UnionOf | `anyOf: [schemas]` |
| IntersectionOf | `allOf: [schemas]` |
| xsd:string | `type: string` |
| xsd:integer | `type: integer` |
| xsd:dateTime | `type: string, format: date-time` |
| xsd:anyURI | `type: string, format: uri` |
| Object to resource | `x-grl-model-link: ResourceName` |
| Taxonomy | `enum: [individual labels]` |

---

## Execution Order

1. **Create docs/ structure** - Empty files with headers
2. **Write configuration documentation** - config-reference.md, templates.md, security.md
3. **Write ontology design documentation** - restrictions.md, datatypes.md, annotations.md
4. **Write examples** - minimal-example.md, common-patterns.md
5. **Create test ontologies** - One per feature area
6. **Create test configs** - One per ontology
7. **Implement integration tests** - Follow documentation systematically

---

## Verification

1. **Documentation**: Review each doc file covers its source file completely
2. **Ontologies**: Run with `-G` flag to verify namespace flattening
3. **Tests**: Run `mvn verify` to execute all integration tests
4. **Coverage**: Ensure each documented feature has a corresponding test

```bash
# Build and verify
mvn clean verify

# Run specific test class
mvn test -Dtest=CardinalityRestrictionsIT

# Run all feature tests
mvn verify -Dit.test="regression.features.**"
```


If you need specific details from before exiting plan mode (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /home/mike/.claude/projects/-home-mike-grl-ASG/43e167cf-7801-44df-a774-8f3acf52b348.jsonl

content.text: I'll implement this comprehensive documentation and integration test plan. Let me start by reading the key source files to understand the current implementation, then create the documentation structure and tests.

content.content:
1→# [ASG](https://github.com/Graph-Research-Labs/ASG)
     2→
     3→API Specification Generator (ASG) is a tool that generates OpenAPI specification files from an ontology. The tool is
     4→designed to generate OpenAPI specification files for APIs that are based on ontologies. The tool is designed to be run
     5→from the command line or can be integrated into a java application/maven build.
     6→In turn these specification files can be used to generate API documentation, client and server code, and other tools
     7→that can be used to interact with the API.
     8→
     9→## Prerequisites:
    10→
    11→1. Java 21 or later.
    12→2. Maven 3.6.3 or later.
    13→3. An ontology in OWL RDF format.
    14→4. A configuration file in YAML format.
    15→5. An XML catalog file to resolve ontology imports (optional).
    16→
    17→## Build steps:
    18→
    19→1. `mvn clean package` to compile and package the jars.
    20→2. `mvn clean install` to compile and install jars in your local maven repository.
    21→2. `mvn clean deploy` to deploy the jars to the remote repository.
    22→
    23→The target directory will contain the following jars:
    24→
    25→1. asg-<revision>.jar - The main jar file for use when embedding the tool in a java application.
    26→2. asg-<revision>-jar-with-dependencies.jar - The executable jar file with all dependencies included.
    27→3. asg-<revision>-sources.jar - The source jar file.
    28→
    29→## How to run from the command line:
    30→
    31→1. `java -jar asg-<revision>-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c <config-file>` to run the executable jar file.
    32→
    33→## Jar may be copied from maven repository:
    34→
    35→`mvn dependency:copy -Dartifact=com.grl.component:asg:0.0.4-SNAPSHOT:jar:jar-with-dependencies -DoverWriteIfNewer=true -DoutputDirectory=.`
    36→
    37→## How to run as part of a maven build:
    38→
    39→1. Add the following dependency to your pom.xml file:
    40→
    41→```xml
    42→
    43→<dependency>
    44→    <groupId>com.graphresearchlabs</groupId>
    45→    <artifactId>asg</artifactId>
    46→    <version>0.0.4</version>
    47→</dependency>
    48→```
    49→
    50→2. Add the following plugin to your pom.xml file:
    51→
    52→```xml  
    53→
    54→<groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
    55→<artifactId>exec-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    56→<version>1.6.0</version>
    57→<goal>java</goal>
    58→<configuration>
    59→<mainClass>com.grl.component.asg.Asg</mainClass>
    60→<arguments>
    61→    <argument>-c</argument>
    62→    <argument>${path to config}</argument>
    63→</arguments>
    64→</configuration>
    65→```
    66→
    67→## Getting Started
    68→
    69→Briefly:
    70→
    71→1. An ontology must be available as an owl RDF file.
    72→2. A configuration file must be created to specify the output directory, ontology file and configuration options.
    73→3. Run the executable jar file with the configuration file as an argument.
    74→4. OpenApi specification files (yaml and json) will be generated in the output directory.
    75→
    76→## Configuration File
    77→
    78→The yaml file consists of the following fields:
    79→
    80→* `name`: (required) The name of the API. This will be used as a namespace when identifying this API amongst others.
    81→* `ontologies`: (required) A list of ontology file(s)/uri(s). If there are multiple files, they will be merged into a
    82→  single ontology.
    83→* `outputDir`: (required) The directory where the OpenApi specification files will be generated. Note that a
    84→  subdirectory with the name of the API will be created in this directory.
    85→* `rootClass`: (optional) IRI of the root class from which all resources in the API are subclassed with
    86→  hasResourceSchema. If not defined will default to the only single class that is a subclass of APIRoot. If more than
    87→  one, this value is required.
    88→* `extends`: (optional) The path to another configuration file to extend. The fields in the extended configuration file
    89→  will be merged with the fields in the current configuration file. The fields in the current configuration file will
    90→  take precedence. Lists will be concatenated, maps will be merged.
    91→* `namespaceMappings`: (optional) A URIs to be flatten into. This is used to map IRIs to flatten
    92→  single
    93→  namespace. The values are arrays or regexps to match namespaces to be flattened. Regexps may be
    94→  optionally preceded by '!' to specify exclusion
    95→* `catalogFile`: (optional) The location of an XML catalog file to resolve ontology imports.
    96→* `securitySchemaName`: (optional) name of the security scheme to use for the generated api. If not specified, the first
    97→  oauth2 security scheme found in the openapi template will be used
    98→* `enforceRequiredness`" (optional) If true, properties which have minCardinality/exact > 0 or some will be added to
    99→  `required` sets on owning objects in the JSON schema
   100→* `defaultSecurityTemplate` (optional) Template with placeholders that will be used when no security annotations are
   101→  present` to generate security requirements. Placeholders
   102→  are as follows:
   103→* {api} the name of the api (config.name)
   104→  * {action} mapped for each http method being generated from the config.securityActionMappings
   105→  * {model} The resource (model) name for each api resource being generated
   106→    A null or empty defaultSecurityTemplate will suppress generation of any security specification
   107→    for individual paths when no security annotation is present
   108→* `securityTemplate` (optional, defaulted) Template with placeholders that will be used with the security annotations on
   109→  a resource to generate security requirements
   110→  * {security} the security string as obtained from the "security" annotation. Other place holders will be replaced as
   111→    above.
   112→    A null or empty securityTemplate will suppress generation of any security specification for
   113→    individual paths when a security annotations is present
   114→* `templates`: (optional) root of templates as follows:
   115→    * `openApi`: (optional) top level [OpenAPI](https://swagger.io/specification/#openapi-object) which will have
   116→      generated values merged in
   117→    * `collectionPathItem`: (optional) Template the [PathItem](https://swagger.io/specification/#path-item-object) for a
   118→      collection api operations (.g. GET /<resource>). Generated spec will be merged over this for each resource's
   119→      collection operations.
   120→    * `singlePathItem`: (optional) Template the [PathItem](https://swagger.io/specification/#path-item-object) for a
   121→      single resource api operations (.g. GET /<resource>/<id>). Generated spec will be merged over
   122→
   123→All relative paths and uris are interpreted relative to the location of the configuration file they appear in.
   124→
   125→Defaults are read from the src/main/resources/default.config.yaml resource as if this were an implied global 'extends'
   126→config.
   127→
   128→## Example Configuration File
   129→
   130→```yaml
   131→ontologies:
   132→  - ../Insurance Common Data Record API/Insurance Core Data Record API Ontology-1.0.4.owl
   133→name: insuranceCDR
   134→extends: base-config.yaml
   135→catalogFile: ../Insurance Common Data Record API/catalog-v001.xml
   136→namespaceMappings:
   137→  https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/InsuranceCDR/:
   138→    - https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/.*
   139→    - https://ontologies.semanticarts.com/.*
   140→    - https://w3id.org/semanticarts/ns/ontology/gist/.*
   141→rootClass: https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/InsuranceCDR/InsuranceCDRAPIV1.0.0
   142→templates:
   143→  openapi:
   144→    info:
   145→      description: Insurance API
   146→      title: Insurance API
   147→    servers:
   148→      - url: insurance
   149→        description: description
   150→```
   151→
   152→This uses a base-config.yaml to supply some values which would be shared by other configs in the project:
   153→
   154→```yaml
   155→outputDir: target
   156→templates:
   157→  openapi:
   158→    openapi: 3.1.0
   159→    info:
   160→      version: 1.0.1
   161→      termsOfService: terms
   162→      contact:
   163→        name: Dougal Watt
   164→        url: http://www.climatetracker.com/
   165→        email: support@graphresearchlabs.com
   166→      license:
   167→        name: Copyright 2024 Graphic Research Labs
   168→        url: http://www.climatetracker.com/
   169→    components:
   170→      securitySchemes:
   171→        oauth2:
   172→          type: oauth2
   173→          flows:
   174→            authorizationCode:
   175→              authorizationUrl: https://dummy
   176→              tokenUrl: https://dummy
   177→
   178→    externalDocs:

   181→```
   182→## Example Output

   187→│   ├── effective-config.yaml (merged configuration file, with extends file and paths etc resolved recursively) 
   188→│   ├── ontology0.owl (each ontology file is copied to the output directory)
   189→│   ├── ontology-flat.owl (ontology file with namespace mappings flattened)
   190→│   ├── ontology-merged.owl (ontology file with all imports merged)
   191→│   ├── openapi.json (generated OpenAPI specification file JSON)
   192→│   └── openapi.yaml (generated OpenAPI specification file YAML)
   193→````
   194→
   195→## Ontology design
   196→
   197→To successfully run ASG, an ontology must be supplied that supports the Graph Research Labs annotation and structural ontology requirements. These requirements are applied to an existing ontology, and create a graph view on top of the ontology suitable for generating a YAML specification.
   198→

   200→
   201→Structure the ontology
   202→1. Create your ontology by first importing the Graph Research Labs 'base' ontology. This will provide the minimal ontology structure required for ASG and includes structural classes and properties that are critical and cannot be removed:
   203→- 'API Root' class - this links to a class containing the api specification you will generate with ASG, via the 'has api' object property. This class should be subclassed off 'Specification Entry', and contain as it's URI the shortened named and version number of the API (to allow for multiple versions if required). For example: https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/employment/EmploymentAPIV1.0.1

   205→  'has api' - links API Root to the API specification to be generated

For example, " 'has resource schema' exactly 1 'Disclose Climate Related Impacts' " will generate GET, PUT, POST, PATCH and DELETE API resources, and the associated schema elements that are linked as triples from the 'Disclose Climate Related Impacts' class.
   207→
   208→Apply annotations
   209→2. The Graph Research Labs 'base' ontology will import an annotation model called 'Annotation Properties Fragment', which constains the required annotations under the 'grl annotation properties' annotation property:
   210→- 'api description' - applied to an API resource, this annotation determines the full description shown for a given path in the generated YAML 
   211→- 'api summary' - applied to an API resource, this annotation determines the summary description shown for a given path in the generated YAML
   212→- 'api tag' -  - applied to an API resource, this annotation creates one or more tags used to organise and group paths in the generated YAML

Nested schema can be created by applying this annotation to triples for subclassess. For example, adding this annotation/value to the triple 'has climate related capital deployment metric' exactly 1 'Capital Deployment Amount Metric' and its properties ensures they will all be included in the generated API schema.

file.content:
# [ASG](https://github.com/Graph-Research-Labs/ASG)

API Specification Generator (ASG) is a tool that generates OpenAPI specification files from an ontology. The tool is
designed to generate OpenAPI specification files for APIs that are based on ontologies. The tool is designed to be run
from the command line or can be integrated into a java application/maven build.
In turn these specification files can be used to generate API documentation, client and server code, and other tools
that can be used to interact with the API.

## Prerequisites:

1. Java 21 or later.
2. Maven 3.6.3 or later.
3. An ontology in OWL RDF format.
4. A configuration file in YAML format.
5. An XML catalog file to resolve ontology imports (optional).

## Build steps:

1. `mvn clean package` to compile and package the jars.
2. `mvn clean install` to compile and install jars in your local maven repository.
2. `mvn clean deploy` to deploy the jars to the remote repository.

The target directory will contain the following jars:

1. asg-<revision>.jar - The main jar file for use when embedding the tool in a java application.
2. asg-<revision>-jar-with-dependencies.jar - The executable jar file with all dependencies included.
3. asg-<revision>-sources.jar - The source jar file.

## How to run from the command line:

1. `java -jar asg-<revision>-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c <config-file>` to run the executable jar file.

## Jar may be copied from maven repository:

`mvn dependency:copy -Dartifact=com.grl.component:asg:0.0.4-SNAPSHOT:jar:jar-with-dependencies -DoverWriteIfNewer=true -DoutputDirectory=.`

## How to run as part of a maven build:

1. Add the following dependency to your pom.xml file:

```xml

<dependency>
    <groupId>com.graphresearchlabs</groupId>
    <artifactId>asg</artifactId>
    <version>0.0.4</version>
</dependency>
```

2. Add the following plugin to your pom.xml file:

```xml  

<groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
<artifactId>exec-maven-plugin</artifactId>
<version>1.6.0</version>
<goal>java</goal>
<configuration>
<mainClass>com.grl.component.asg.Asg</mainClass>
<arguments>
    <argument>-c</argument>
    <argument>${path to config}</argument>
</arguments>
</configuration>
```

## Getting Started

Briefly:

1. An ontology must be available as an owl RDF file.
2. A configuration file must be created to specify the output directory, ontology file and configuration options.
3. Run the executable jar file with the configuration file as an argument.
4. OpenApi specification files (yaml and json) will be generated in the output directory.

## Configuration File

The yaml file consists of the following fields:

* `name`: (required) The name of the API. This will be used as a namespace when identifying this API amongst others.
* `ontologies`: (required) A list of ontology file(s)/uri(s). If there are multiple files, they will be merged into a
  single ontology.
* `outputDir`: (required) The directory where the OpenApi specification files will be generated. Note that a
  subdirectory with the name of the API will be created in this directory.
* `rootClass`: (optional) IRI of the root class from which all resources in the API are subclassed with
  hasResourceSchema. If not defined will default to the only single class that is a subclass of APIRoot. If more than
  one, this value is required.
* `extends`: (optional) The path to another configuration file to extend. The fields in the extended configuration file
  will be merged with the fields in the current configuration file. The fields in the current configuration file will
  take precedence. Lists will be concatenated, maps will be merged.
* `namespaceMappings`: (optional) A URIs to be flatten into. This is used to map IRIs to flatten
  single
  namespace. The values are arrays or regexps to match namespaces to be flattened. Regexps may be
  optionally preceded by '!' to specify exclusion
* `catalogFile`: (optional) The location of an XML catalog file to resolve ontology imports.
* `securitySchemaName`: (optional) name of the security scheme to use for the generated api. If not specified, the first
  oauth2 security scheme found in the openapi template will be used
* `enforceRequiredness`" (optional) If true, properties which have minCardinality/exact > 0 or some will be added to
  `required` sets on owning objects in the JSON schema
* `defaultSecurityTemplate` (optional) Template with placeholders that will be used when no security annotations are
  present` to generate security requirements. Placeholders
  are as follows:
* {api} the name of the api (config.name)
  * {action} mapped for each http method being generated from the config.securityActionMappings
  * {model} The resource (model) name for each api resource being generated
    A null or empty defaultSecurityTemplate will suppress generation of any security specification
    for individual paths when no security annotation is present
* `securityTemplate` (optional, defaulted) Template with placeholders that will be used with the security annotations on
  a resource to generate security requirements
  * {security} the security string as obtained from the "security" annotation. Other place holders will be replaced as
    above.
    A null or empty securityTemplate will suppress generation of any security specification for
    individual paths when a security annotations is present
* `templates`: (optional) root of templates as follows:
    * `openApi`: (optional) top level [OpenAPI](https://swagger.io/specification/#openapi-object) which will have
      generated values merged in
    * `collectionPathItem`: (optional) Template the [PathItem](https://swagger.io/specification/#path-item-object) for a
      collection api operations (.g. GET /<resource>). Generated spec will be merged over this for each resource's
      collection operations.
    * `singlePathItem`: (optional) Template the [PathItem](https://swagger.io/specification/#path-item-object) for a
      single resource api operations (.g. GET /<resource>/<id>). Generated spec will be merged over

All relative paths and uris are interpreted relative to the location of the configuration file they appear in.

Defaults are read from the src/main/resources/default.config.yaml resource as if this were an implied global 'extends'
config.

## Example Configuration File

```yaml
ontologies:
  - ../Insurance Common Data Record API/Insurance Core Data Record API Ontology-1.0.4.owl
name: insuranceCDR
extends: base-config.yaml
catalogFile: ../Insurance Common Data Record API/catalog-v001.xml
namespaceMappings:
  https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/InsuranceCDR/:
    - https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/.*
    - https://ontologies.semanticarts.com/.*
    - https://w3id.org/semanticarts/ns/ontology/gist/.*
rootClass: https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/InsuranceCDR/InsuranceCDRAPIV1.0.0
templates:
  openapi:
    info:
      description: Insurance API
      title: Insurance API
    servers:
      - url: insurance
        description: description
```

This uses a base-config.yaml to supply some values which would be shared by other configs in the project:

```yaml
outputDir: target
templates:
  openapi:
    openapi: 3.1.0
    info:
      version: 1.0.1
      termsOfService: terms
      contact:
        name: Dougal Watt
        url: http://www.climatetracker.com/
        email: support@graphresearchlabs.com
      license:
        name: Copyright 2024 Graphic Research Labs
        url: http://www.climatetracker.com/
    components:
      securitySchemes:
        oauth2:
          type: oauth2
          flows:
            authorizationCode:
              authorizationUrl: https://dummy
              tokenUrl: https://dummy

    externalDocs:
      description: External Documentation
      url: http://www.climatetracker.com/
```
## Example Output

```terminal
output
├── insuranceCDR
│   ├── effective-config.yaml (merged configuration file, with extends file and paths etc resolved recursively) 
│   ├── ontology0.owl (each ontology file is copied to the output directory)
│   ├── ontology-flat.owl (ontology file with namespace mappings flattened)
│   ├── ontology-merged.owl (ontology file with all imports merged)
│   ├── openapi.json (generated OpenAPI specification file JSON)
│   └── openapi.yaml (generated OpenAPI specification file YAML)
````

## Ontology design

To successfully run ASG, an ontology must be supplied that supports the Graph Research Labs annotation and structural ontology requirements. These requirements are applied to an existing ontology, and create a graph view on top of the ontology suitable for generating a YAML specification.

The process for creating an ASG-ready ontology is as follows:

Structure the ontology
1. Create your ontology by first importing the Graph Research Labs 'base' ontology. This will provide the minimal ontology structure required for ASG and includes structural classes and properties that are critical and cannot be removed:
- 'API Root' class - this links to a class containing the api specification you will generate with ASG, via the 'has api' object property. This class should be subclassed off 'Specification Entry', and contain as it's URI the shortened named and version number of the API (to allow for multiple versions if required). For example: https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/employment/EmploymentAPIV1.0.1
- the set of object properties used in constructing the API, located as sub-properties of 'api object properties'
  'has api' - links API Root to the API specification to be generated
  'has resource schema' - defines the API resources that will be generated by ASG, as links to one or more classes. For example, " 'has resource schema' exactly 1 'Disclose Climate Related Impacts' " will generate GET, PUT, POST, PATCH and DELETE API resources, and the associated schema elements that are linked as triples from the 'Disclose Climate Related Impacts' class.

Apply annotations
2. The Graph Research Labs 'base' ontology will import an annotation model called 'Annotation Properties Fragment', which constains the required annotations under the 'grl annotation properties' annotation property:
- 'api description' - applied to an API resource, this annotation determines the full description shown for a given path in the generated YAML 
- 'api summary' - applied to an API resource, this annotation determines the summary description shown for a given path in the generated YAML
- 'api tag' -  - applied to an API resource, this annotation creates one or more tags used to organise and group paths in the generated YAML
- 'api label' - applied to classes linked via the 'has resource schema', this annotation specifies what triples will be included in the resource schema attached to an API path, using the URI suffix of the API being generated as the value of the annotation e.g. as per the example above "EmploymentAPIV1.0.1". Nested schema can be created by applying this annotation to triples for subclassess. For example, adding this annotation/value to the triple 'has climate related capital deployment metric' exactly 1 'Capital Deployment Amount Metric' and its properties ensures they will all be included in the generated API schema.

input.content:
# ASG Documentation

API Specification Generator (ASG) generates OpenAPI 3.1 specification files from OWL-RDF ontologies. It transforms semantic ontology structures into REST API specifications that can be used to generate API documentation, client and server code, and other tools.

## Quick Links

- [Getting Started](getting-started.md) - 5-minute setup guide
- [CLI Reference](cli-reference.md) - Command-line options
- [Configuration Reference](configuration/config-reference.md) - All YAML configuration options

## Documentation Sections

### Configuration

- [Configuration Overview](configuration/index.md) - How configuration works
- [Configuration Reference](configuration/config-reference.md) - Complete YAML option reference
- [Templates](configuration/templates.md) - OpenAPI template customization
- [Security](configuration/security.md) - Security scheme configuration

### Ontology Design

- [Ontology Requirements](ontology-design/index.md) - What your ontology needs
- [Structure](ontology-design/structure.md) - Required structure (APIRoot, resources)
- [Annotations](ontology-design/annotations.md) - All supported annotations
- [Restrictions](ontology-design/restrictions.md) - OWL restrictions to OpenAPI mapping
- [Datatypes](ontology-design/datatypes.md) - XSD to OpenAPI type mapping

### Mapping Reference

- [OWL to OpenAPI Matrix](mapping-reference/owl-to-openapi-matrix.md) - Quick reference
- [Property Mappings](mapping-reference/property-mappings.md) - Data/Object/Taxonomy properties

### Examples

- [Minimal Example](examples/minimal-example.md) - Simplest working configuration
- [Common Patterns](examples/common-patterns.md) - Recipe-style patterns

### Troubleshooting

- [Troubleshooting Guide](troubleshooting.md) - Common issues and solutions

## Prerequisites

- Java 21 or later
- Maven 3.6.3 or later
- An ontology in OWL RDF format
- A configuration file in YAML format

## Output

ASG generates the following files in the output directory:

```
output/<api-name>/
├── effective-config.yaml    # Merged configuration
├── ontology0.owl           # Original ontology copy
├── ontology-flat.owl       # Namespace-flattened
├── ontology-merged.owl     # All imports merged
├── openapi.json            # Generated spec (JSON)
└── openapi.yaml            # Generated spec (YAML)
```

input.content:
# Getting Started

This guide will help you generate your first OpenAPI specification from an OWL ontology in under 5 minutes.

## Prerequisites

1. Java 21 or later installed
2. ASG JAR file (either build from source or download)

## Build ASG (if needed)

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Graph-Research-Labs/ASG.git
cd ASG
mvn clean package -DskipTests
```

The executable JAR will be at `target/asg-<version>-jar-with-dependencies.jar`.

## Step 1: Create Your Configuration

Create a file named `config.yaml`:

```yaml
# Required: Name of your API (alphanumeric with underscores)
name: myapi

# Required: Path to your ontology file
ontologies:
  - ./my-ontology.owl

# Required: Output directory for generated files
outputDir: ./output

# Required: The root class that defines your API resources
rootClass: https://example.com/ontology/MyAPIV1.0

# Recommended: Enable required field enforcement
enforceRequiredness: true

# OpenAPI template customization
templates:
  openapi:
    openapi: 3.1.0
    info:
      title: My API
      version: 1.0.0
      description: Generated from OWL ontology
```

## Step 2: Prepare Your Ontology

Your ontology must follow the ASG structure. At minimum, you need:

1. **An API root class** that is a subclass of `APIRoot` with a `hasAPI` restriction
2. **Resource classes** linked via `hasResourceSchema`
3. **Properties** annotated with `apiLabel` matching your API name

See [Ontology Structure](ontology-design/structure.md) for detailed requirements.

## Step 3: Run ASG

```bash
java -jar asg-<version>-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c config.yaml
```

## Step 4: Check Output

Your generated OpenAPI specification will be in:
- `output/myapi/openapi.yaml` (YAML format)
- `output/myapi/openapi.json` (JSON format)

## What's Generated

For each resource class in your ontology, ASG generates:

| Endpoint | Methods | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `/{Resource}` | GET, POST | Collection endpoint (list, create) |
| `/{Resource}/{id}` | GET, PUT, PATCH, DELETE | Single resource endpoint |

Each resource also gets a schema in `components/schemas/` with properties derived from your ontology's data and object properties.

## Next Steps

- [CLI Reference](cli-reference.md) - All command-line options
- [Configuration Reference](configuration/config-reference.md) - Complete YAML options
- [Ontology Structure](ontology-design/structure.md) - Detailed ontology requirements
- [Minimal Example](examples/minimal-example.md) - Complete working example

input.content:
# CLI Reference

ASG is run from the command line using the executable JAR.

## Basic Usage

```bash
java -jar asg-<version>-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c <config-file>
```

## Options

| Option | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `-c <file>` | **Required.** Path to the YAML configuration file |
| `-G` | Stop after flattening the ontology (debug mode). Useful for verifying namespace mappings |

## Examples

### Generate API Specification

```bash
java -jar asg-1.0.0-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c config.yaml
```

### Debug Mode (Stop After Flattening)

```bash
java -jar asg-1.0.0-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c config.yaml -G
```

This stops processing after the ontology flattening step and outputs `ontology-flat.owl`. Use this to verify that namespace mappings are working correctly before full generation.

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | Error (configuration, ontology loading, or generation failure) |

## Running from Maven

ASG can also be run as part of a Maven build:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
    <artifactId>exec-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>1.6.0</version>
    <configuration>
        <mainClass>com.grl.component.asg.Asg</mainClass>
        <arguments>
            <argument>-c</argument>
            <argument>${project.basedir}/config.yaml</argument>
        </arguments>
    </configuration>
</plugin>
```

Then run with:

```bash
mvn exec:java
```

## Programmatic Usage

ASG can be embedded in Java applications:

```java
import com.grl.component.asg.Asg;

public class MyApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Asg.main(new String[]{"-c", "config.yaml"});
    }
}
```

Add the dependency:

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.graphresearchlabs</groupId>
    <artifactId>asg</artifactId>
    <version>1.0.0</version>
</dependency>
```

```bash
java -jar asg-1.0.0-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c config.yaml
```

### Debug Mode (Stop After Flattening)

```bash
java -jar asg-1.0.0-jar-with-dependencies.jar -c config.yaml -G
```

This stops processing after the ontology flattening step and outputs `ontology-flat.owl`. Use this to verify that namespace mappings are working correctly before full generation.

## Exit Codes

| Code | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| 0 | Success |
| 1 | Error (configuration, ontology loading, or generation failure) |

## Running from Maven

ASG can also be run as part of a Maven build:

```xml
<plugin>
    <groupId>org.codehaus.mojo</groupId>
    <artifactId>exec-maven-plugin</artifactId>
    <version>1.6.0</version>
    <configuration>
        <mainClass>com.grl.component.asg.Asg</mainClass>
        <arguments>
            <argument>-c</argument>
            <argument>${project.basedir}/config.yaml</argument>
        </arguments>
    </configuration>
</plugin>
```

Then run with:

```bash
mvn exec:java
```

## Programmatic Usage

ASG can be embedded in Java applications:

```java
import com.grl.component.asg.Asg;

public class MyApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Asg.main(new String[]{"-c", "config.yaml"});
    }
}
```

Add the dependency:

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.graphresearchlabs</groupId>
    <artifactId>asg</artifactId>
    <version>1.0.0</version>
</dependency>
```

input.content:
# Configuration Overview

ASG uses YAML configuration files to control the generation process. This section covers how configuration works, including inheritance, variable substitution, and all available options.

## Configuration Files

- [Configuration Reference](config-reference.md) - Complete list of all YAML options
- [Templates](templates.md) - OpenAPI template customization
- [Security](security.md) - Security scheme configuration

## Basic Structure

A minimal configuration file:

```yaml
name: myapi
ontologies:
  - ./ontology.owl
outputDir: ./output
rootClass: https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0
```

## Configuration Inheritance

Configuration files can extend other files using the `extends` field:

```yaml
# base-config.yaml
outputDir: ./output
enforceRequiredness: true
templates:
  openapi:
    openapi: 3.1.0
```

```yaml
# api-config.yaml
extends: base-config.yaml
name: myapi
ontologies:
  - ./ontology.owl
rootClass: https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0
```

### Merge Behavior

- **Scalar values**: Child overrides parent
- **Lists**: Child list replaces parent list entirely
- **Maps/Objects**: Deep merge (child keys override, parent keys preserved)

## Variable Substitution

Configuration files support variable substitution:

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `${configDir}` | Directory containing the configuration file |

Example:

```yaml
ontologies:
  - ${configDir}/../ontologies/my-ontology.owl
outputDir: ${configDir}/../../output
catalogFile: ${configDir}/../ontologies/catalog.xml
```

## Path Resolution

All relative paths are resolved relative to the configuration file's location, not the current working directory.

## Default Configuration

ASG loads default values from an internal `default.config.yaml` resource. This acts as an implicit base configuration that all configs extend.

## Effective Configuration

After generation, the merged/effective configuration is written to `effective-config.yaml` in the output directory. This is useful for debugging inheritance and variable substitution.

input.content:
# Configuration Reference

Complete reference for all YAML configuration options.

## Required Fields

### `name`

**Type:** `string` (alphanumeric and underscores only)

The name of the API being generated. Used as:
- Output subdirectory name
- Namespace identifier
- Part of operation IDs

```yaml
name: myapi
```

### `ontologies`

**Type:** `list of URI/path strings`

List of ontology files to load. Multiple files are merged into a single ontology.

```yaml
ontologies:
  - ./my-ontology.owl
  - ./extensions.owl
```

Supports:
- Relative paths (resolved from config file location)
- Absolute paths
- URIs (`file://`, `http://`, `https://`)

### `outputDir`

**Type:** `path`

Directory where generated files will be written. A subdirectory named after `name` will be created.

```yaml
outputDir: ./output
```

Output structure:
```
output/myapi/
├── effective-config.yaml
├── ontology0.owl
├── ontology-flat.owl
├── ontology-merged.owl
├── openapi.json
└── openapi.yaml
```

## Optional Fields

### `extends`

**Type:** `path`

Path to another configuration file to extend. Fields are merged recursively.

```yaml
extends: ../base-config.yaml
```

See [Configuration Overview](index.md) for merge behavior details.

### `rootClass`

**Type:** `IRI string`

The IRI of the root class that defines the API resources. Required if multiple classes are subclasses of `APIRoot`.

```yaml
rootClass: https://example.com/ontology/MyAPIV1.0
```

If not specified, ASG infers the root from the single class that is a subclass of `APIRoot`.

### `catalogFile`

**Type:** `path`

Path to an XML catalog file for resolving ontology imports.

```yaml
catalogFile: ./catalog-v001.xml
```

### `enforceRequiredness`

**Type:** `boolean`
**Default:** `false`

When `true`, properties with cardinality restrictions (`minCardinality >= 1`, `exactCardinality >= 1`, or `someValuesFrom`) are added to the `required` array in the JSON Schema.

```yaml
enforceRequiredness: true
```

### `namespaceMappings`

**Type:** `map` or `list`

Defines how IRIs are flattened into a single namespace. Values are regex patterns to match source namespaces.

**As a list** (target namespace inferred from first ontology):

```yaml
namespaceMappings:
  - "!http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#.*"     # Exclude OWL namespace
  - "!http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#.*"  # Exclude XSD namespace
  - ".*"                                      # Include everything else
```

**As a map** (explicit target namespace):

```yaml
namespaceMappings:
  https://example.com/api/:
    - https://example.com/.*
    - https://other.com/.*
```

Patterns prefixed with `!` are exclusions.

### `securitySchemeName`

**Type:** `string`

Name of the security scheme to use. If not specified, the first OAuth2 scheme found in the OpenAPI template is used.

```yaml
securitySchemeName: oauth2
```

Supports placeholders: `{api}`, `{action}`, `{model}`, `{security}`

### `defaultSecurityTemplate`

**Type:** `string`

Template for generating security requirements when no `security` annotation is present on a resource.

```yaml
defaultSecurityTemplate: "{api}:{model}:{action}"
```

**Placeholders:**
- `{api}` - The API name from config
- `{action}` - HTTP method mapped via `securityActionMappings`
- `{model}` - The resource name

Set to `null` or empty to suppress security generation for unannotated resources.

### `securityTemplate`

**Type:** `string`

Template for generating security requirements when a `security` annotation IS present.

```yaml
securityTemplate: "{security}"
```

**Placeholders:**
- `{security}` - The value from the `security` annotation
- `{api}`, `{action}`, `{model}` - Same as above

### `securityActionMappings`

**Type:** `map`

Maps HTTP methods to action names for use in security templates.

```yaml
securityActionMappings:
  GET: read
  POST: create
  PUT: update
  PATCH: update
  DELETE: delete
```

### `templates`

**Type:** `object`

OpenAPI template objects. See [Templates](templates.md) for details.

```yaml
templates:
  openapi:
    openapi: 3.1.0
    info:
      title: My API
      version: 1.0.0
  collectionPathItem:
    # PathItem template for collection endpoints
  singlePathItem:
    # PathItem template for single-resource endpoints
```

## Complete Example

```yaml
extends: base-config.yaml

name: employment_api

ontologies:
  - ${configDir}/ontologies/employment.owl
  - ${configDir}/ontologies/extensions.owl

outputDir: ${configDir}/../../output

catalogFile: ${configDir}/ontologies/catalog.xml

rootClass: https://example.com/employment/EmploymentAPIV1.0

enforceRequiredness: true

namespaceMappings:
  https://example.com/employment/:
    - "!http://www.w3.org/.*"
    - ".*"

securitySchemeName: oauth2
defaultSecurityTemplate: "{api}.{model}.{action}"
securityActionMappings:
  GET: read
  POST: create
  PUT: update
  PATCH: update
  DELETE: delete

templates:
  openapi:
    openapi: 3.1.0
    info:
      title: Employment API
      version: 1.0.0
      description: HR management API
      contact:
        name: API Support
        email: support@example.com
    servers:
      - url: https://api.example.com/v1
        description: Production
    components:
      securitySchemes:
        oauth2:
          type: oauth2
          flows:
            authorizationCode:
              authorizationUrl: https://auth.example.com/authorize
              tokenUrl: https://auth.example.com/token
```

# OpenAPI Templates

ASG uses templates to provide default values for the generated OpenAPI specification. Generated content is merged over these templates.

## Template Structure

```yaml
templates:
  openapi:      # Top-level OpenAPI object template
    ...
  collectionPathItem:  # Template for collection endpoints (/{Resource})
    ...
  singlePathItem:      # Template for single endpoints (/{Resource}/{id})
    ...
```

## OpenAPI Template

The `openapi` template provides the base structure for the generated specification.

```yaml
templates:
  openapi:
    openapi: 3.1.0
    info:
      title: My API
      version: 1.0.0
      description: API description
      termsOfService: https://example.com/terms
      contact:
        name: API Support
        url: https://example.com/support
        email: support@example.com
      license:
        name: Apache 2.0
        url: https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
    servers:
      - url: https://api.example.com/v1
        description: Production server
      - url: https://staging-api.example.com/v1
        description: Staging server
    externalDocs:
      description: API Documentation
      url: https://docs.example.com
    components:
      securitySchemes:
        oauth2:
          type: oauth2
          flows:
            authorizationCode:
              authorizationUrl: https://auth.example.com/authorize
              tokenUrl: https://auth.example.com/token
              scopes:
                read: Read access
                write: Write access
```

### Generated Content

ASG generates and merges the following:

- `info.title` - From the API root class label
- `info.x-grl-api-label` - API label
- `info.x-grl-graph-namespace` - Target namespace IRI
- `tags` - One per resource with descriptions
- `paths` - All resource endpoints
- `components.schemas` - All resource schemas

## Collection Path Item Template

Template for collection endpoints (`/{Resource}`).

```yaml
templates:
  collectionPathItem:
    summary: Resource collection
    description: Operations on the resource collection
    get:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Success
        '400':
          description: Bad Request
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
      parameters:
        - name: start
          in: query
          description: Index of start item
          schema:
            type: integer
        - name: limit
          in: query
          description: Maximum items to return
          schema:
            type: integer
    post:
      responses:
        '201':
          description: Created
        '400':
          description: Bad Request
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
```

### Generated Collection Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `GET` | List resources (with pagination) |
| `POST` | Create a new resource |

## Single Path Item Template

Template for single-resource endpoints (`/{Resource}/{id}`).

```yaml
templates:
  singlePathItem:
    summary: Single resource
    description: Operations on a single resource
    get:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Existing resource
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '404':
          description: Not Found
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
    put:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Existing resource
        '400':
          description: Bad Request
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '404':
          description: Not Found
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
    patch:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Existing resource
        '400':
          description: Bad Request
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '404':
          description: Not Found
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
    delete:
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Existing resource
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '404':
          description: Not Found
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
```

### Generated Single Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource by ID |
| `PUT` | Replace a resource entirely |
| `PATCH` | Update a resource partially |
| `DELETE` | Delete a resource |

## Merge Behavior

Templates are merged with generated content using deep merge:

1. Generated values override template values for the same key
2. Template values are preserved if not generated
3. Arrays are replaced entirely (not merged)
4. Objects are merged recursively

## Default Template

ASG includes a built-in default template at `src/main/resources/default.config.yaml`. This provides sensible defaults for response codes and standard parameters.

```yaml
      responses:
        '200':
          description: Existing resource
        '401':
          description: Unauthorized
        '403':
          description: Forbidden
        '404':
          description: Not Found
        '406':
          description: Not Acceptable
        '429':
          description: Too Many Requests
        '500':
          description: Internal Server Error
```

### Generated Single Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource by ID |
| `PUT` | Replace a resource entirely |
| `PATCH` | Update a resource partially |
| `DELETE` | Delete a resource |

## Merge Behavior

Templates are merged with generated content using deep merge:

1. Generated values override template values for the same key
2. Template values are preserved if not generated
3. Arrays are replaced entirely (not merged)
4. Objects are merged recursively

## Default Template

ASG includes a built-in default template at `src/main/resources/default.config.yaml`. This provides sensible defaults for response codes and standard parameters.

input.content:
# Security Configuration

ASG supports generating security requirements for OpenAPI operations based on security schemes defined in templates and security annotations on ontology resources.

## Defining Security Schemes

Security schemes are defined in the OpenAPI template:

```yaml
templates:
  openapi:
    components:
      securitySchemes:
        oauth2:
          type: oauth2
          flows:
            authorizationCode:
              authorizationUrl: https://auth.example.com/authorize
              tokenUrl: https://auth.example.com/token
              scopes:
                read: Read access
                write: Write access
                admin: Admin access
        api_key:
          type: apiKey
          in: header
          name: X-API-Key
```

## Security Scheme Selection

### Automatic Selection

If `securitySchemeName` is not specified, ASG automatically selects:

1. The first OAuth2 security scheme found, OR
2. The first API key security scheme found

### Explicit Selection

```yaml
securitySchemeName: oauth2
```

## Security Templates

Security requirements are generated using templates with placeholder substitution.

### Default Security Template

Used when resources do NOT have a `security` annotation:

```yaml
defaultSecurityTemplate: "{api}.{model}.{action}"
```

**Placeholders:**

| Placeholder | Description | Example |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| `{api}` | API name from config | `myapi` |
| `{model}` | Resource class name | `Employee` |
| `{action}` | Mapped HTTP method | `read` |

### Security Template

Used when resources HAVE a `security` annotation:

```yaml
securityTemplate: "{security}"
```

**Additional placeholder:**

| Placeholder | Description | Example |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| `{security}` | Value from security annotation | `admin` |

### Suppressing Security Generation

Set templates to `null` or empty string to suppress security:

```yaml
# No security for unannotated resources
defaultSecurityTemplate: null

# No security even for annotated resources
securityTemplate: null
```

## Action Mappings

Map HTTP methods to action names for use in templates:

```yaml
securityActionMappings:
  GET: read
  POST: create
  PUT: update
  PATCH: update
  DELETE: delete
```

These mappings are used when expanding the `{action}` placeholder.

## Ontology Security Annotations

Add security requirements to specific resources using the `security` annotation:

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/AdminResource">
    <www:security>admin</www:security>
</owl:Class>
```

With `securityTemplate: "{api}.{security}"`, this generates:
```yaml
security:
  - oauth2:
      - myapi.admin
```

## Example Configurations

### OAuth2 with Scopes per Action

```yaml
securitySchemeName: oauth2
defaultSecurityTemplate: "{api}:{model}:{action}"
securityActionMappings:
  GET: read
  POST: write
  PUT: write
  PATCH: write
  DELETE: delete
```

Generated security for `GET /Employee`:
```yaml
security:
  - oauth2:
      - myapi:Employee:read
```

### API Key Only

```yaml
templates:
  openapi:
    components:
      securitySchemes:
        api_key:
          type: apiKey
          in: header
          name: X-API-Key

# No scope-based security templates needed
defaultSecurityTemplate: null
```

### Resource-Level Security

```yaml
securityTemplate: "{api}.{security}"
defaultSecurityTemplate: "{api}.{model}.{action}"
```

Resources with `security` annotation use their custom scope; others use the default pattern.

input.content:
# Ontology Design Overview

To generate an OpenAPI specification, your ontology must follow specific structural patterns that ASG recognizes. This section covers all requirements for creating ASG-compatible ontologies.

## Documentation

- [Structure](structure.md) - Required structure (APIRoot, resources, properties)
- [Annotations](annotations.md) - All supported annotations
- [Restrictions](restrictions.md) - OWL restrictions and their OpenAPI mappings
- [Datatypes](datatypes.md) - XSD to OpenAPI type mapping

## Key Concepts

### API Identification

ASG identifies an API by finding a class that is a subclass of `APIRoot` with a `hasAPI` restriction. This class defines which resources are exposed in the API.

### Resource Classes

Resources are classes that become REST endpoints. Each resource class:
- Is linked via `hasResourceSchema` from the API class
- Has an `apiID` data property for unique identification
- Generates collection (`/Resource`) and single (`/Resource/{id}`) endpoints

### Property Mapping

Properties on resource classes become schema properties:

| OWL Property Type | OpenAPI Mapping |
|-------------------|-----------------|
| Data Property | Primitive type (string, number, etc.) |
| Object Property to Resource | `$ref` with `x-grl-model-link` |
| Object Property to Non-Resource | Inline object schema |
| Object Property with Individuals | Enum (taxonomy) |

### Annotation-Based Filtering

ASG uses the `apiLabel` annotation to filter which axioms are included in the generated API. Only restrictions annotated with the matching API label are processed.

## Required Imports

Your ontology should import the GRL base ontology which provides:

- `APIRoot` class
- `hasAPI` object property
- `hasResourceSchema` object property
- `apiID` data property
- Annotation properties

```xml
<owl:imports rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/V1.0.2"/>
```

## Minimal Example

```xml
<!-- API Root -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0">
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/GRLAPISpecifications"/>
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/hasResourceSchema"/>
            <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Resource Class -->
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <!-- Annotated restriction for apiID -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/apiID"/>
            <owl:qualifiedCardinality>1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
            <owl:onDataRange rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>
    <!-- Annotated restriction for name property -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/name"/>
            <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Annotation linking restriction to API -->
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- blank node reference --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apiLabel>MyAPIV1.0</www:apiLabel>
</owl:Axiom>
```

input.content:
# Ontology Structure

This document describes the required structure for ASG-compatible ontologies.

## API Identification

### APIRoot Class

Every API must be identified through a class hierarchy that connects to `APIRoot`:

1. **APIRoot** is a well-known class in the base ontology
2. Your API specification class must be reachable from `APIRoot` via `hasAPI`

```xml
<!-- APIRoot links to your API spec -->
<rdf:Description rdf:about="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/APIRoot">
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/hasAPI"/>
            <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>
</rdf:Description>
```

### API Specification Class

The API specification class defines which resources are exposed:

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0">
    <!-- Extend GRLAPISpecifications -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/GRLAPISpecifications"/>

    <!-- Link to resources via hasResourceSchema -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/hasResourceSchema"/>
            <owl:qualifiedCardinality>1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
            <owl:onClass rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>

    <!-- Display label -->
    <skos:prefLabel>My API V1.0</skos:prefLabel>
    <GRLAnnotations:apiDisplayLabel>MyAPIV1.0</GRLAnnotations:apiDisplayLabel>
</owl:Class>
```

### Multiple APIs

If your ontology contains multiple API specification classes, you must specify which one to use via the `rootClass` configuration:

```yaml
rootClass: https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0
```

## Resource Classes

Resource classes become REST API resources with full CRUD operations.

### Requirements

Each resource class must:

1. Be linked from the API spec via `hasResourceSchema`
2. Have an `apiID` data property (for unique identification)
3. Have properties annotated with `apiLabel` matching the API name

### Generated Endpoints

For each resource class (e.g., `Employee`):

| Endpoint | Methods |
|----------|---------|
| `/Employee` | GET (list), POST (create) |
| `/Employee/{EmployeeId}` | GET, PUT, PATCH, DELETE |

### Example Resource Class

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <!-- Human-readable label -->
    <skos:prefLabel>Employee</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>A person employed by an organization.</skos:definition>

    <!-- Required: apiID property -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/apiID"/>
            <owl:qualifiedCardinality>1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
            <owl:onDataRange rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>

    <!-- Data property: name -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/name"/>
            <owl:qualifiedCardinality>1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
            <owl:onDataRange rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>

    <!-- Object property: works for employer -->
    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/worksFor"/>
            <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employer"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>
</owl:Class>

<!-- Annotations linking to API -->
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- blank node for apiID restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apiLabel>MyAPIV1.0</www:apiLabel>
</owl:Axiom>
<!-- Similar axioms for other restrictions... -->
```

## Property Annotations

Properties must be annotated with `apiLabel` to be included in the generated schema.

### Axiom Annotations

The `apiLabel` annotation goes on the SubClassOf axiom, not the property itself:

```xml
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- blank node reference to the restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apiLabel>MyAPIV1.0</www:apiLabel>
</owl:Axiom>
```

This allows the same property to be included in different APIs with different configurations.

## Nested Resources

Object properties pointing to other resource classes generate references:

```xml
<!-- Employee has an employer (also a resource) -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/employer"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employer"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

Generated schema:
```yaml
employer:
  type: string
  x-grl-model-link: Employer
  description: Unique identifier of Employer
  example: Employer-12345
```

## Non-Resource Object Properties

Object properties pointing to classes that are NOT resources become inline objects:

```xml
<!-- Address is not a resource (no apiID) -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/address"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/Address"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

The `Address` class schema is generated inline or as a `$ref` without `x-grl-model-link`.

## Tag Annotations

Use `apiTag` on the `hasResourceSchema` axiom to organize resources:

```xml
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- hasResourceSchema restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apitag>Employees</www:apitag>
</owl:Axiom>
```

This adds the resource to the specified tag group in the OpenAPI specification.

```xml
<!-- Employee has an employer (also a resource) -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/employer"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employer"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

Generated schema:
```yaml
employer:
  type: string
  x-grl-model-link: Employer
  description: Unique identifier of Employer
  example: Employer-12345
```

## Non-Resource Object Properties

Object properties pointing to classes that are NOT resources become inline objects:

```xml
<!-- Address is not a resource (no apiID) -->
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/address"/>
        <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="https://example.com/Address"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

The `Address` class schema is generated inline or as a `$ref` without `x-grl-model-link`.

## Tag Annotations

Use `apiTag` on the `hasResourceSchema` axiom to organize resources:

```xml
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- hasResourceSchema restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apitag>Employees</www:apitag>
</owl:Axiom>
```

This adds the resource to the specified tag group in the OpenAPI specification.

input.content:
# Annotations

ASG recognizes several annotation properties that control API generation. These come from the GRL annotation ontology and standard vocabularies (SKOS, RDFS).

## GRL Annotation Properties

These annotations are defined in the GRL annotation properties ontology (`https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/GRLAnnotations/`).

### apiLabel

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/apiLabel`

Marks a SubClassOf axiom as belonging to a specific API. Only axioms with matching `apiLabel` are included in the generated schema.

**Usage:** Applied to the axiom (owl:Axiom), not the class or property directly.

```xml
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- restriction blank node --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apiLabel>MyAPIV1.0</www:apiLabel>
</owl:Axiom>
```

**Effect:** Includes the annotated restriction in the API schema.

### apiDescription

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/GRLAnnotations/apiDescription`

Provides the full description for an API path operation.

**Usage:** Applied to resource classes.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <GRLAnnotations:apiDescription>
        Manages employee records including personal information,
        employment history, and organizational assignments.
    </GRLAnnotations:apiDescription>
</owl:Class>
```

**Effect:** Sets the `description` field on path operations.

### apiSummary

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/GRLAnnotations/apisummary`

Provides a short summary for an API path.

**Usage:** Applied to resource classes.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <GRLAnnotations:apisummary>Employee management</GRLAnnotations:apisummary>
</owl:Class>
```

**Effect:** Sets the `summary` field on path items.

### apiTag

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/GRLAnnotations/apitag`

Assigns a resource to a tag group for organization in API documentation.

**Usage:** Applied to the hasResourceSchema axiom.

```xml
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- hasResourceSchema restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apitag>Human Resources</www:apitag>
</owl:Axiom>
```

**Effect:** Adds the resource to the specified tag in `tags` and operations.

### apiDisplayLabel

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/GRLAnnotations/apiDisplayLabel`

Display name for the API specification.

**Usage:** Applied to the API specification class.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/MyAPIV1.0">
    <GRLAnnotations:apiDisplayLabel>MyAPIV1.0</GRLAnnotations:apiDisplayLabel>
</owl:Class>
```

**Effect:** Sets the `x-grl-api-label` extension and influences titles.

### apiID

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/apiID`

The data property that holds the unique identifier for resource instances.

**Usage:** As a data property restriction on resource classes.

```xml
<rdfs:subClassOf>
    <owl:Restriction>
        <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/base/apiID"/>
        <owl:qualifiedCardinality>1</owl:qualifiedCardinality>
        <owl:onDataRange rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
    </owl:Restriction>
</rdfs:subClassOf>
```

**Effect:**
- Marks the class as a REST resource
- Creates the `apiID` property in the schema
- Generates the `{ResourceId}` path parameter

### security

**IRI:** `https://www.graphresearchlabs.com/GRLAnnotations/security`

Specifies security requirements for a resource.

**Usage:** Applied to resource classes.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/AdminResource">
    <GRLAnnotations:security>admin</GRLAnnotations:security>
</owl:Class>
```

**Effect:** Used with `securityTemplate` to generate OAuth2 scopes or other security requirements.

## SKOS Annotations

Standard SKOS vocabulary annotations for labels and definitions.

### skos:prefLabel

**IRI:** `http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#prefLabel`

Preferred human-readable label.

**Usage:** Applied to classes and properties.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <skos:prefLabel>Employee</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:Class>

<owl:DatatypeProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/firstName">
    <skos:prefLabel>First Name</skos:prefLabel>
</owl:DatatypeProperty>
```

**Effect:** Sets the `title` field on schemas and properties.

### skos:definition

**IRI:** `http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#definition`

Full definition or description.

**Usage:** Applied to classes and properties.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <skos:definition>A person who is employed by an organization.</skos:definition>
</owl:Class>
```

**Effect:** Sets the `description` field on schemas.

### skos:example

**IRI:** `http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#example`

Example value for a property.

**Usage:** Applied to data properties or their restriction axioms.

```xml
<owl:DatatypeProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/email">
    <skos:example>john.doe@example.com</skos:example>
</owl:DatatypeProperty>
```

Or on the axiom for context-specific examples:

```xml
<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- email restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <skos:example>employee@company.com</skos:example>
</owl:Axiom>
```

**Effect:** Sets the `example` field on properties.

## RDFS Annotations

### rdfs:comment

**IRI:** `http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#comment`

Fallback description when skos:definition is not present.

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <rdfs:comment>Represents an employee in the system.</rdfs:comment>
</owl:Class>
```

**Effect:** Used as `description` if no `skos:definition` is present.

## Annotation Priority

When multiple annotations provide similar information, ASG uses this priority:

### For Labels/Titles
1. `skos:prefLabel`
2. `rdfs:label`
3. IRI short form

### For Descriptions
1. `skos:definition`
2. `rdfs:comment`
3. `apiDescription` (for paths)

### For Examples
1. `skos:example` on restriction axiom
2. `skos:example` on property
3. Auto-generated based on datatype

## Complete Annotation Example

```xml
<owl:Class rdf:about="https://example.com/Employee">
    <skos:prefLabel>Employee</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>A person who is employed by an organization.</skos:definition>
    <GRLAnnotations:apisummary>Employee management</GRLAnnotations:apisummary>
    <GRLAnnotations:apiDescription>
        Full CRUD operations for managing employee records.
    </GRLAnnotations:apiDescription>
    <GRLAnnotations:security>hr.employees</GRLAnnotations:security>

    <rdfs:subClassOf>
        <owl:Restriction>
            <owl:onProperty rdf:resource="https://example.com/email"/>
            <owl:someValuesFrom rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#string"/>
        </owl:Restriction>
    </rdfs:subClassOf>
</owl:Class>

<owl:Axiom>
    <owl:annotatedSource rdf:resource="https://example.com/Employee"/>
    <owl:annotatedProperty rdf:resource="http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#subClassOf"/>
    <owl:annotatedTarget><!-- email restriction --></owl:annotatedTarget>
    <www:apiLabel>MyAPIV1.0</www:apiLabel>
    <skos:example>employee@company.com</skos:example>
</owl:Axiom>

<owl:DatatypeProperty rdf:about="https://example.com/email">
    <skos:prefLabel>Email Address</skos:prefLabel>
    <skos:definition>The employee's work email address.</skos:definition>
</owl:DatatypeProperty>
```
